title: Polymorphic Ventricular Tachycardia and Ventricular Fibrillation
authors: William H. Sauer, Usha B. Tedrow

items.subtopic: Polymorphic Ventricular Tachycardia and Ventricular Fibrillation | POLYMORPHIC VENTRICULAR TACHYCARDIA | POLYMORPHIC VT ASSOCIATED WITH ACUTE MYOCARDIAL INFARCTION/ISCHEMIA
items.title: Management of Polymorphic VT in Acute Myocardial Infarction
items.content: Polymorphic ventricular tachycardia (VT) associated with acute myocardial infarction or ischemia is often due to reentry through the infarct border zone. The risk of developing VT that degenerates into VF is highest in the first hour post-MI, affecting about 10% of patients.
keyPoints: Approximately 10% of acute MI patients develop polymorphic VT progressing to VF.
The greatest risk period for this complication is within the first hour of an acute MI.
Management includes defibrillation per ACLS guidelines, $\beta$-adrenergic blockers, electrolyte correction, and prompt myocardial reperfusion.
Repeated episodes may indicate inadequate myocardial reperfusion.
In-hospital mortality is higher if polymorphic VT or VF occurs within the first 48 hours of MI.
items.reference: Polymorphic Ventricular Tachycardia and Ventricular Fibrillation · Polymorphic Ventricular Tachycardia and Ventricular Fibrillation | POLYMORPHIC VENTRICULAR TACHYCARDIA | POLYMORPHIC VT ASSOCIATED WITH ACUTE MYOCARDIAL INFARCTION/ISCHEMIA

items.subtopic: Polymorphic Ventricular Tachycardia and Ventricular Fibrillation | POLYMORPHIC VENTRICULAR TACHYCARDIA | POLYMORPHIC VT ASSOCIATED WITH ACUTE MYOCARDIAL INFARCTION/ISCHEMIA
items.title: Long-term Management of Postinfarct Ventricular Arrhythmias
items.content: For patients with postinfarct ventricular arrhythmia, long-term management depends on residual left ventricular function. An implantable cardioverter defibrillator (ICD) is recommended for those with persistent severe LV dysfunction.
keyPoints: Long-term therapy is guided by residual left ventricular function.
An ICD is indicated if the left ventricular ejection fraction is <35% due to persistent severe dysfunction.
Patients surviving past hospital discharge are not at increased risk for arrhythmic sudden death.
items.reference: Polymorphic Ventricular Tachycardia and Ventricular Fibrillation · Polymorphic Ventricular Tachycardia and Ventricular Fibrillation | POLYMORPHIC VENTRICULAR TACHYCARDIA | POLYMORPHIC VT ASSOCIATED WITH ACUTE MYOCARDIAL INFARCTION/ISCHEMIA

items.subtopic: ACQUIRED LONG QT SYNDROME
items.title: Management of Acquired Long QT Syndrome
items.content: Acquired Long QT Syndrome is characterized by a pause-dependent initiation sequence leading to polymorphic ventricular tachycardia (VT) and can progress to ventricular fibrillation (VF). Management includes intravenous magnesium sulfate, increasing heart rate with isoproterenol or pacing, correcting electrolyte disturbances, and avoiding medications that prolong the QT interval.
keyPoints: Pause-dependent initiation sequence of polymorphic VT
Causes include electrolyte abnormalities, bradycardia, and certain medications (e.g., sotalol, dofetilide, ibutilide)
Management: IV magnesium sulfate 1–2 g; if ineffective, increase heart rate to 100-120 depolarizations/min with isoproterenol or pacing
Correct electrolyte disturbances (hypokalemia and hypocalcemia) and remove causative drugs
Avoid future exposure to QT-prolonging medications
items.reference: Polymorphic Ventricular Tachycardia and Ventricular Fibrillation · ACQUIRED LONG QT SYNDROME | CONGENITAL LONG QT SYNDROME

items.subtopic: CONGENITAL LONG QT SYNDROME
items.title: Diagnostic Criteria for Congenital Long QT Syndrome
items.content: Congenital Long QT Syndrome is due to genetic mutations affecting cardiac ion channels, leading to prolonged corrected QT interval (QTc). Diagnostic thresholds are >440 ms in men and 460 ms in women. It manifests with symptoms from torsades des pointes VT.
keyPoints: Caused by mutations in genes coding for cardiac ion channels
Corrected QT (QTc) is typically prolonged to >440 ms in men and 460 ms in women
Symptoms include torsades des pointes VT
Three identified forms: LQTS-1, LQTS-2, LQTS-3
items.reference: Polymorphic Ventricular Tachycardia and Ventricular Fibrillation · ACQUIRED LONG QT SYNDROME | CONGENITAL LONG QT SYNDROME

items.subtopic: TABLE 262-1 Causes of QT Prolongation and Torsades des Pointes | SHORT QT SYNDROME | BRUGADA SYNDROME
items.title: Characteristics and Management of Long QT Syndrome (LQTS)
items.content: Long QT syndrome is primarily caused by mutations affecting potassium, sodium, or calcium channels. It typically presents with syncope or cardiac arrest during childhood. LQTS-1 episodes are often triggered by exertion, especially swimming; LQTS-2 by auditory stimuli or emotional upset; and LQTS-3 during sleep. Beta-blockers like nadolol and propranolol are the mainstay of treatment for most patients with LQTS-1 or LQTS-2. Avoidance of QT-prolonging drugs is crucial.
keyPoints: 90% of cases due to mutations in potassium, sodium, or calcium channels
LQTS-1: episodes during exertion (swimming); LQTS-2: sudden auditory stimuli/emotional upset; LQTS-3: during sleep
Beta-blockers (nadolol, propranolol) are primary treatment for LQTS-1 and LQTS-2
QTc interval >500 ms, female gender, history of syncope/cardiac arrest indicate increased risk
Avoidance of QT-prolonging drugs is essential
items.reference: Polymorphic Ventricular Tachycardia and Ventricular Fibrillation · TABLE 262-1 Causes of QT Prolongation and Torsades des Pointes | SHORT QT SYNDROME | BRUGADA SYNDROME

items.subtopic: TABLE 262-1 Causes of QT Prolongation and Torsades des Pointes | SHORT QT SYNDROME | BRUGADA SYNDROME
items.title: Understanding Short QT Syndrome
items.content: Short QT syndrome is a rare condition characterized by an abnormally short QTc interval (<360 ms, usually <300 ms). It involves gain of function in potassium channels or reduced inward depolarizing currents. This syndrome is associated with atrial fibrillation, polymorphic VT, and sudden death.
keyPoints: Short QT syndrome is rare compared to LQTS
QTc interval <360 ms, usually <300 ms
Associated with atrial fibrillation, polymorphic VT, and sudden death
items.reference: Polymorphic Ventricular Tachycardia and Ventricular Fibrillation · TABLE 262-1 Causes of QT Prolongation and Torsades des Pointes | SHORT QT SYNDROME | BRUGADA SYNDROME

items.subtopic: TABLE 262-1 Causes of QT Prolongation and Torsades des Pointes | SHORT QT SYNDROME | BRUGADA SYNDROME
items.title: Diagnosis and Treatment of Brugada Syndrome
items.content: Brugada syndrome is characterized by ST-segment elevation (>0.2 mV) in anterior precordial leads (V1-V3), often leading to syncope or cardiac arrest due to polymorphic VT without structural heart disease. It shows autosomal dominant inheritance with variable penetrance and involves mutations in sodium channels (~25% of cases). Diagnosis can be aided by sodium channel-blocking drugs like flecainide, ajmaline, or procainamide. An ICD is recommended for patients with unexplained syncope or resuscitated from cardiac arrest.
keyPoints: ST-segment elevation >0.2 mV in V1-V3
Syncope/cardiac arrest due to polymorphic VT without structural heart disease
~25% of cases have mutations in sodium channels (SCN5A, SCN1B, SCN10A)
ICD indicated for unexplained syncope or post-resuscitation from cardiac arrest
Avoid drugs affecting sodium channels; quinidine and catheter ablation can be used to manage VT
items.reference: Polymorphic Ventricular Tachycardia and Ventricular Fibrillation · TABLE 262-1 Causes of QT Prolongation and Torsades des Pointes | SHORT QT SYNDROME | BRUGADA SYNDROME

items.subtopic: EARLY REPOLARIZATION SYNDROME | CATECHOLAMINERGIC POLYMORPHIC VT | HYPERTROPHIC CARDIOMYOPATHY
items.title: Management of Early Repolarization Syndrome with J-point Elevation
items.content: In patients resuscitated from VF without structural heart disease, the presence of J-point elevation suggests early repolarization syndrome. This condition may have a genetic basis and is associated with arrhythmias. An ICD is recommended for those who have experienced prior cardiac arrest.
keyPoints: Higher prevalence of J-point elevation in patients resuscitated from VF without structural heart disease
Potential genetic basis suggested by family history of sudden death
J-point elevation also seen in Brugada syndrome, associated with higher arrhythmia risk
ICD recommended for those with prior cardiac arrest
items.reference: Polymorphic Ventricular Tachycardia and Ventricular Fibrillation · EARLY REPOLARIZATION SYNDROME | CATECHOLAMINERGIC POLYMORPHIC VT | HYPERTROPHIC CARDIOMYOPATHY

items.subtopic: EARLY REPOLARIZATION SYNDROME | CATECHOLAMINERGIC POLYMORPHIC VT | HYPERTROPHIC CARDIOMYOPATHY
items.title: Risk Factors and Management of Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy (HCM)
items.content: HCM is the most common genetic cardiovascular disorder, with a prevalence of 1 in 500 individuals. It is a significant cause of sudden death before age 35. Risk factors for sudden death include young age, nonsustained VT, failure of blood pressure to increase during exercise, recent syncope, ventricular wall thickness >3 cm, and severity of LV outflow obstruction. An ICD is generally indicated for high-risk patients.
keyPoints: HCM prevalence: 1 in 500 individuals
Prominent cause of sudden death before age 35
Risk factors for sudden death: young age, nonsustained VT, failure of blood pressure increase during exercise, recent syncope, ventricular wall thickness >3 cm, severity of LV outflow obstruction
ICD generally indicated for high-risk subjects
items.reference: Polymorphic Ventricular Tachycardia and Ventricular Fibrillation · EARLY REPOLARIZATION SYNDROME | CATECHOLAMINERGIC POLYMORPHIC VT | HYPERTROPHIC CARDIOMYOPATHY

items.subtopic: GENETIC DILATED CARDIOMYOPATHIES | VENTRICULAR FIBRILLATION
items.title: Indications for ICD in Genetic Dilated Cardiomyopathies
items.content: Patients with genetic dilated cardiomyopathies, particularly those with mutations in Lamin A/C or SCN5A genes, are at increased risk of ventricular arrhythmias. Implantable cardioverter-defibrillators (ICDs) are recommended for patients who have experienced sustained VT, exhibit significantly depressed ventricular function (LV ejection fraction <35%), or have a family history of sudden death.
keyPoints: Genetic dilated cardiomyopathies account for 30–40% of nonischemic cases.
Mutations in Lamin A/C and SCN5A genes are linked to conduction system disease and ventricular arrhythmias.
ICDs recommended for sustained VT, LV ejection fraction <35%, or malignant family history of sudden death.
items.reference: Polymorphic Ventricular Tachycardia and Ventricular Fibrillation · GENETIC DILATED CARDIOMYOPATHIES | VENTRICULAR FIBRILLATION | FUTURE DIRECTIONS

items.subtopic: VENTRICULAR FIBRILLATION | FUTURE DIRECTIONS
items.title: Management and Future Directions in Ventricular Fibrillation
items.content: Ventricular fibrillation (VF) involves disordered electrical activation, often leading to cardiac arrest. Treatment follows ACLS guidelines with defibrillation. Post-resuscitation evaluation is crucial for identifying underlying causes such as monomorphic or polymorphic VT. If reversible causes like acute MI are not found, ICD therapy is recommended. Future directions include catheter ablation using simultaneous electrical recordings and detailed imaging to target arrhythmia sites.
keyPoints: VF characterized by disordered ventricular activation without identifiable QRS complexes.
Treatment involves ACLS guidelines with defibrillation.
Post-resuscitation evaluation for underlying causes, including VT initiation of VF.
ICD therapy recommended if no reversible cause is identified.
Future directions: catheter ablation using electrical recordings and cardiac imaging.
items.reference: Polymorphic Ventricular Tachycardia and Ventricular Fibrillation · GENETIC DILATED CARDIOMYOPATHIES | VENTRICULAR FIBRILLATION | FUTURE DIRECTIONS

items.subtopic: ACQUIRED LONG QT SYNDROME | CONGENITAL LONG QT SYNDROME
items.question: What is a characteristic initiation sequence of polymorphic VT in acquired long QT syndrome?
options: A sinus beat followed by a premature ventricular contraction
A pause-dependent premature ventricular beat followed by a longer QT interval sinus beat
A rapid succession of sinus beats
items.explanation: The initiation sequence is characterized by a pause-dependent premature ventricular beat followed by a sinus beat with a longer QT interval.

options: Intravenous administration of 1–2 g of magnesium sulphate
items.explanation: Magnesium sulphate is used to suppress recurrent episodes.

items.question: What is the typical ECG finding in Brugada syndrome?
options: >0.2 mV of ST-segment depression with a covered ST segment
>0.2 mV of ST-segment elevation with a covered ST segment and negative T wave
<0.1 mV of ST-segment elevation with positive T wave
ST-segment elevation in the inferior leads
items.explanation: Brugada syndrome is characterized by >0.2 mV of ST-segment elevation with a covered ST segment and negative T wave.

items.assertion: The characteristic ST-segment elevation in Brugada syndrome can become pronounced during acute illness.
